video_title: The Next Billion Crypto Users Won't Be Human
gist: David, founder of Pharos (Faros), an EVM-compatible financial L1, argues that sustainable RWA growth must come from real institutional-grade cash-flowing assets ('RealFi') rather than incentive-looped stablecoins, and that the next wave of on-chain users and capital will be AI agents and Web2 retail/unbanked populations served via specialized, vertically integrated chains rather than one monolithic chain.

insights.claim: Pharos's founder argues most current RWA is unsustainable because it is looped risky assets wrapped across protocols to stack incentives, which amplifies risk rather than creating real value.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Signals skepticism toward yield-bearing/looped RWA products; watch for blowups in heavily re-hypothecated RWA vaults as a downside catalyst.
insights.quote: Um it What What they really do is amplifying the risk. So, um I don't think that type of RWA is is sustainable.

insights.claim: RWA is not new and has recurred across cycles since the 2016-17 ICO era through DeFi, but no self-sustaining use case has yet driven organic growth.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Frames RWA as a perennially over-hyped narrative; investors should demand evidence of organic, non-incentivized adoption before underwriting RWA plays.
insights.quote: RWA has is not a new thing, first of all. It has been exist for couple cycles. Starting properly from like 2016-17 from the ICOs.

insights.claim: RealFi is defined as combining institutional-grade risk management, responsible partners, and real users addressing daily financial needs, using real assets with real cash flow rather than incentive farming.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: A diligence lens for evaluating RWA/on-chain credit: prioritize underlying cash flow and continuous risk monitoring over headline APY.
insights.quote: It's a basically a combination of institutional grade of risk management, it's responsive partners, it's basically the real users that come here

insights.claim: High-quality institutional assets (e.g. top asset managers' fund products investing in quality credit instruments or company equity) sit idle on-chain because they are harder to structure and require rigorous diligence compared to stablecoins.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Identifies the supply-side bottleneck and opportunity in tokenizing complex institutional assets; structuring/diligence infra is the moat to watch.
insights.quote: there is a lot of high-quality institutional assets has sitting idle and hasn't been really used to create use cases.

insights.claim: Multi-wrapping of RWA tokens across protocols compounds risk, so any inadequately diligenced underlying asset becomes a time bomb once re-hypothecated.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Re-hypothecation depth is a hidden systemic risk metric for RWA exposure; map how many wraps deep a position sits before sizing it.
insights.quote: After couple wraps the risk amplified and then if the underlying assets hasn't been rigorously diligence and then continuously monitored is basically time bomb waiting to fall

themes.theme: Agents as the next user base

insights.claim: Pharos's founder predicts that in the near term a meaningful share, and in the longer term the majority, of on-chain users will be AI agents rather than humans.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: If agentic transaction volume dominates, value accrues to chains/infra optimized for machine users (low latency, verifiable execution, unified data), not UI-centric apps.
insights.quote: In the near future, maybe majority of the users will be agents.

insights.claim: Serving agents requires fundamentally different infrastructure than humans: no UI, but a unified database, verifiable and fast execution, high-quality LLMs, and pre-built agents.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Distinguishes 'agent-ready' chains; verifiable execution and machine-readable state become competitive differentiators for capturing agentic flows.
insights.quote: they don't need a UI. They they need a unified database. They need a the execution that can be verified.

insights.claim: Pharos positions itself as 'agent-fi' with agents already conducting live transactions on its mainnet, and cites an x402-style payment-request layer (referred to as 'XFlow 2') that agents interact with to initiate payments.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Early live agentic payment use cases are an adoption signal; agent-payment standards are an emerging infra layer to track for investment.
insights.quote: we have builders that actually be building the agents, building the services to provide it to the agents on Firo's mainnet today.

insights.claim: Pharos is building a 'vault economy' by recruiting curators and vault infra providers to structure, launch and co-market vaults on-chain, and launched a 'p-alpha' vault a few weeks before the recording.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Vaults are the dominant permissionless RWA wrapper; chains competing for curator supply and GTM support are a leading indicator of RWA TVL migration.
insights.quote: we launched a couple weeks ago. And then they will take the p-alpha token uh and then started to add started to build their customized invest uh strategy.

insights.claim: Pharos wants LPs to move from holding vault tokens to maturity toward actively re-using them (leveraging/looping p-alpha for higher yield, or de-risking by blending with safer assets), turning investors into issuers.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Encouraging looping/leverage of RWA tokens to grow TVL reintroduces the re-hypothecation risk the founder warns against elsewhere; a tension worth flagging.
insights.quote: I hope they don't just hold to maturity. They will hold to use. Um they themselves turn them in turn themselves from a investor also into a issuer.

insights.claim: Crypto-native vault assets carry lockup periods of T+30, T+60 or T+90, making secondary markets and lending markets essential to provide holders liquidity.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Lockups create demand for RWA secondary liquidity and collateralized lending; liquidity venues for locked RWA tokens are an underbuilt opportunity.
insights.quote: some of them are T+30, some of them are T+60, some of T+90.

themes.theme: Chain architecture, tech stack and trade-offs

insights.claim: Pharos is an EVM-compatible L1 claiming over 100k TPS currently, theoretically up to 300k, with sub-100-millisecond latency, targeting programmable-money settlement for neo-banks.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Performance claims position Pharos against high-throughput chains for payment/settlement workloads; verify TPS claims against real mainnet load before crediting them.
insights.quote: We are a ultra high performance chain, uh, where we have over 100k, um, TPS at this moment. We we can theoretically go up to 300 300k. Uh, we have a less than 100 milliseconds latency.

insights.claim: Pharos is built on an 'engine' tech stack that has existed since 2016 powering private/consortium chains for large financial institutions, now being applied to a public chain for the first time.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Reuse of battle-tested enterprise/consortium tech is a credibility differentiator vs greenfield L1s; the specific stack lineage is worth diligencing.
insights.quote: this is built on top a tech stack a lot of people not familiar with but it's basically engine tax stack. The engine tax stack actually exists since 2016.

insights.claim: Pharos deliberately excludes GameFi and SocialFi and avoids ultra-high-volume institutional settlement, accepting slower growth and narrower reach as the trade-off for a hyper-focus on high-quality financial assets.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Specialization thesis: narrow vertical focus over general-purpose; growth metrics will lag broad L1s, so judge it on RWA-specific adoption.
insights.quote: you probably won't find game five, social five, right? ... We are a relatively narrowed and hyper-focused on financial use cases.

insights.claim: Pharos's founder believes the industry has settled the 'one chain vs a million chains' debate in favor of many specialized chains, and that this fragmentation trend will not reverse into a single jumbo chain.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Bets against consolidation; the moat is per-chain specialization, implying value accrues to differentiated niche chains and interoperability layers.
insights.quote: Everybody has been debating whether there would be a million chains or there would be one chain ... And apparently it's the latter.

insights.claim: Pharos sees continued roles for Ethereum L1 (citing upcoming performance upgrades from Vitalik and the Ethereum team that could make mainnet suitable for financial use cases) and Solana (large established community growing at the fastest pace).
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Non-zero-sum view of incumbents; Ethereum scaling upgrades are flagged as a potential catalyst for institutional/financial use cases on mainnet.

insights.claim: Pharos describes itself as vertically integrated and non-competitive with builders, operating its own tokenization platform and risk-curation capabilities while also partnering with third-party tokenization platforms and curators ('frenemies').
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Owning competing capabilities while courting partners is a structural conflict to watch; ecosystem neutrality claims should be tested against in-house products.
insights.quote: we have a lot of so called frenemy right so they are also the friend friends but they're also the enemies

insights.claim: Pharos's founder states a Clarity Act passed the day before recording, which he expects to pave the way for a crowded wave of US neo-banks by providing regulatory certainty.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Regulatory clarity is framed as the unlock for US crypto neo-banks; expect a surge of new entrants competing for payment/programmable-money infra.
insights.quote: I mean Clarity Act passed yesterday and then that basically paved the way for the neo banks particularly in US.

insights.claim: Neo-banks are aggregators bundling payment rails with crypto, tokenized equities and programmable money, and that programmable money needs a place to issue, settle and circulate that Pharos aims to capture.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Settlement layer for neo-bank programmable money is the prize; whoever wins neo-bank integrations captures recurring transaction flow.

themes.theme: Adoption reality check and market timing

insights.claim: Pharos's founder argues the industry is overestimating institutional adoption, which he says is a real trend but 'not there yet,' partly because frequent on-chain security incidents deter risk-averse institutions.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Tempers bullish institutional-adoption theses; positions investors to expect a 6-12+ month lag rather than imminent trillion-dollar inflows.
insights.quote: There's a people that believe there's a lot of institutional adoptions that happening like right now ... I totally see the trend, but it's not there yet.

insights.claim: On-chain security incidents are increasing because attackers are using AI agents trained to find bugs 24/7, which the founder says will further delay institutional adoption if unaddressed.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: AI-driven exploit discovery is a rising systemic risk; security/audit infra and AI-defense tooling become more valuable as exploit cadence rises.
insights.quote: I think some of them are filled by some AI capabilities now because people actually train the AI agent to be able to find the bugs like 24/7.

insights.claim: The founder calls expectations of a trillion-dollar institutional inflow within 3-6 months an overestimation, arguing it more likely requires 6-12 months or longer to properly resolve security and use-case issues.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete timeline expectation for institutional capital; near-term inflow disappointment is a risk for adoption-driven valuations.
insights.quote: We'll see maybe like a trillion dollar uh of of growth in the next three months, six months. I think that's overestimation.

insights.claim: The founder argues the industry is underestimating remaining-99% interest and that current attention shift to AI (with people leaving crypto) makes this the ideal time for serious long-term builders, likely fueling a bull market in a year or two.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Classic build-in-the-bear thesis; talent/attention rotation to AI may be a contrarian buy signal for durable crypto infra projects on a 1-2 year horizon.
insights.quote: Maybe some people will see that right now is a better market but not necessarily for builders ... now is the perfect time to build.

insights.claim: Pharos's North Star metric is the number of real use cases addressing genuine business problems, deliberately downweighting common KPIs like TVL, active users and trading volume that can be gamed by incentives.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Signals that headline TVL for this chain should be discounted as a vanity metric; evaluate real cash-flow use cases instead.
insights.quote: my number one like uh key I guess the uh uh measurement is that how many real use cases we have been building.

insights.claim: The founder defines ultimate success as mass adoption of Web2 capital and Web2 users, noting on-chain finance is still under 1% of the overall financial world with room to grow to 5-10% and beyond.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Frames the TAM as the <1% on-chain share of global finance; the investable thesis is the on-ramp of off-chain capital, not rotating existing crypto liquidity.
insights.quote: On chain capital right now today ... if you compare it with the overall financial world, we're still less than 1%.

insights.claim: Pharos targets unbanked Southeast Asian populations who lack bank accounts but rely heavily on apps, requiring a flexible payment rail that serves both institutional (low-fee, heavy KYC/KYB) and retail (friendly, high-fee) on/off ramps cost-efficiently.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Emerging-market unbanked retail is the demand-side bet; affordable on/off-ramp infra for these users is the make-or-break execution risk.
insights.quote: It doesn't really serve, uh for example, unbanked population in Southeast Asia ... they typically don't don't have a bank account, but they heavily relying on their app

insights.claim: Pharos's founder calls inclusive finance a niche but blue-ocean market he expects to grow significantly over the next 12 months, 3 years and decade.
insights.speaker: David (Pharos founder)
insights.so_what: Multi-horizon growth claim for the inclusive-finance niche; a thesis to revisit against actual adoption milestones at 12 months and 3 years.
insights.quote: This is this is a niche market but at the same time this is also a blue ocean market.
